overview.introduction: In Linux, every file and directory has an owner and a set of permissions that control who can read, write, or execute it. Getting permissions wrong is one of the most common causes of deployment failures and security vulnerabilities.
overview.why_it_matters: Misconfigured file permissions are a leading cause of security breaches. A world-writable config file or an SSH key readable by other users can compromise an entire server.
overview.industry_usage: Used by system administrators, DevOps engineers, and security teams to control access to application config files, SSH keys, deployment scripts, and database credentials.
overview.what_you_will_build: You will configure file permissions for a simulated deployment: set correct permissions on config files, scripts, and SSH keys — the way it's done in real production environments.
overview.prerequisites: Linux Command Line Basics — you should know ls, cd, pwd, touch, and mkdir.
learning_outcomes: Read the permission string (e.g., -rwxr-xr--) from ls -l output.
Use chmod with symbolic (u+x) and numeric (755) modes.
Change file ownership using chown and chgrp.
Understand why SSH keys must be permission 600 and directories 700.
Apply correct permissions for web server files, scripts, and configs.

key_concepts.title: File Permissions (r, w, x)
key_concepts.definition: Rules that decide who can read, modify, or execute a file.
key_concepts.explanation: Every file has permissions for three groups: Owner, Group, and Others. You can see these permissions by running ls -l.

commands.name: ls -l
commands.purpose: Lists files showing permissions and owner.
commands.output_explanation: Shows permissions (rw- for owner, r-- for group, r-- for others).

key_concepts.title: chmod (Symbolic Mode)
key_concepts.definition: Changes file permissions using letters to add or remove access.
key_concepts.explanation: Use +x to add execute permission so a script can run. Use go-w to remove write permission from group and others to prevent modifications.

commands.name: chmod +x
commands.output_explanation: Allows deploy.sh to be run with ./deploy.sh.

commands.name: chmod go-w
commands.purpose: Removes write permission from group and others.
commands.output_explanation: Blocks anyone except the owner from writing to secrets.txt.

key_concepts.title: chmod (Numeric Mode)
key_concepts.definition: A quick way to set exact permissions using numbers.
key_concepts.explanation: 600 makes a file private (owner only, e.g. SSH keys). 700 makes a directory private. 755 makes a script runnable by all but editable only by you.

commands.name: chmod 600
commands.purpose: Sets file to owner read/write only.
commands.output_explanation: Locks the file secrets.txt so only the owner can read or edit it.

commands.name: chmod 700
commands.purpose: Sets directory to owner full access only.
commands.output_explanation: Restricts the folder so only the owner can cd into or view it.

commands.name: chmod 755
commands.purpose: Sets script to public executable but private write.
commands.output_explanation: Lets anyone run deploy.sh, but only you can edit it.

key_concepts.title: chown (Ownership)
key_concepts.definition: Changes the user or group who owns a file or folder.
key_concepts.explanation: Every file is owned by a user. Use chown to change who owns a file or folder so services have correct access.

commands.name: chown
commands.output_explanation: Sets the owner of deploy.sh to the user 'student'.

interactive_examples.objective: Check the current permissions of all files in your home directory.
interactive_examples.scenario: Before a security audit, you need to verify that no sensitive files are world-readable.
interactive_examples.explanation: ls -la lists all files (including hidden) in long format, showing permissions, ownership, size, and modification date for every item in the home directory.
interactive_examples.expected_output:
total 32
drwxr-xr-x 4 student student 4096 Jan 12 .
drwxr-xr-x 5 root    root    4096 Jan 12 ..
-rw------- 1 student student  220 Jan 12 .bash_history
-rw-r--r-- 1 student student 3526 Jan 12 .bashrc
drwx------ 2 student student 4096 Jan 12 .ssh
-rw-r--r-- 1 student student   45 Jan 12 welcome.txt
interactive_examples.output_explanation: .ssh directory is drwx------ (700) — private. .bashrc is -rw-r--r-- (644) — readable by all but writable only by owner.
interactive_examples.real_world_use: Run this at the start of a security audit to quickly spot any files with overly permissive access.
interactive_examples.common_mistakes: Not checking the .ssh directory — SSH keys with wrong permissions cause login failures.
interactive_examples.tips: Pay attention to the .ssh directory — it should always be 700, and keys inside should be 600.

interactive_examples.scenario: You just created a deployment script but can't run it — it throws 'Permission denied'.
interactive_examples.explanation: chmod +x adds execute permission. The ls -l after confirms the change — look for x in the permission string.
interactive_examples.output_explanation: The x bits are now set for owner, group, and others. The script can now be executed with ./deploy.sh.
interactive_examples.real_world_use: Every shell script in a CI/CD pipeline must be chmod +x before it can be executed by the runner.
interactive_examples.common_mistakes: Running chmod 777 deploy.sh — gives everyone write access to your script, which is a security risk.
interactive_examples.tips: Use chmod 755 for scripts that should be executable by all but only modified by the owner.

interactive_examples.objective: Protect a sensitive config file from other users.
interactive_examples.scenario: Your .env file contains a database password. You need to restrict access so only you can read it.
interactive_examples.explanation: 600 = rw------- = owner can read and write, group and others have no access at all.
interactive_examples.output_explanation: Only the student user can read or write .env. No one else has any access.
interactive_examples.real_world_use: Set all .env files, private keys, and credential files to 600 to prevent unauthorized access.
interactive_examples.common_mistakes: Leaving credential files at 644 — other users on the system can read your passwords.
interactive_examples.tips: SSH private keys (id_rsa) must be 600 or stricter. SSH will refuse to use them otherwise.

interactive_examples.objective: Change ownership of files to the web server user.
interactive_examples.scenario: You deployed a web app as root. Now nginx can't serve the files because it runs as www-data.
interactive_examples.explanation: chown -R recursively changes all files in /var/www/html/ to be owned by www-data user and group. The web server process can now read and serve them.
interactive_examples.expected_output:
total 24
drwxr-xr-x 2 www-data www-data 4096 Jan 12 .
drwxr-xr-x 4 root     root     4096 Jan 12 ..
-rw-r--r-- 1 www-data www-data 1234 Jan 12 index.html
interactive_examples.output_explanation: index.html is now owned by www-data. The nginx process (running as www-data) can now read and serve it.
interactive_examples.real_world_use: Always chown web files to www-data after deploying to an nginx or Apache server.
interactive_examples.common_mistakes: Forgetting -R and only changing the directory itself, not its contents.
interactive_examples.tips: After chown, also verify permissions with ls -la. Files should be 644, directories 755 for web serving.

interactive_examples.objective: Verify SSH key permissions before connecting to a remote server.
interactive_examples.scenario: You just generated a new SSH key pair and need to ensure the private key has correct permissions.
interactive_examples.explanation: Private key must be 600 (owner read/write only). The .ssh directory must be 700 (owner access only). SSH client enforces these permissions and will fail otherwise.
interactive_examples.expected_output:
total 16
drwx------ 2 student student 4096 Jan 12 .
drwxr-xr-x 5 student student 4096 Jan 12 ..
-rw------- 1 student student 1823 Jan 12 id_rsa
-rw-r--r-- 1 student student  381 Jan 12 id_rsa.pub
interactive_examples.output_explanation: .ssh is 700 (private), id_rsa is 600 (private key), id_rsa.pub is 644 (public key — shareable).
interactive_examples.real_world_use: SSH permissions are enforced by the SSH client. Incorrectly permissioned keys will fail with 'WARNING: UNPROTECTED PRIVATE KEY FILE!'
interactive_examples.common_mistakes: Making id_rsa.pub (public key) private is harmless but making id_rsa (private key) world-readable is a critical security risk.
interactive_examples.tips: The public key (id_rsa.pub) can be 644 — it's meant to be shared. The private key (id_rsa) must be 600 or stricter.

lessons.title: 1. Read Permissions (ls -l)
lessons.definition: Linux stores permission info per file showing who can read, write, or execute.
lessons.explanation: The ls -l command shows permissions in the first column as 10 characters.
lessons.instruction: List files with permissions using ls -l in your home directory.

lessons.title: 2. Create a Script File
lessons.definition: Shell scripts contain commands executed line by line by the bash interpreter.
lessons.explanation: Create a file with .sh extension. It starts as a regular file with no execute permission.
lessons.instruction: Create a file named deploy.sh using touch.

lessons.title: 3. Make Script Executable (chmod +x)
lessons.definition: The execute bit (x) allows a file to be run as a program or script.
lessons.explanation: chmod +x adds execute permission to owner, group, and others.

lessons.title: 4. Restrict File Access (chmod 600)
lessons.definition: Numeric mode 600 means only the owner can read and write — no one else has access.
lessons.explanation: This is the required permission level for SSH private keys and .env secret files.
lessons.instruction: Create a file secrets.txt and set its permissions to 600.

lessons.title: 5. Set Script Permissions (chmod 755)
lessons.definition: Mode 755 gives the owner full access (rwx) and group+others read and execute only.
lessons.explanation: This is the standard permission for executable scripts accessible to all users.

lessons.title: 6. Remove Group Write (chmod go-w)
lessons.definition: Symbolic mode go-w removes write permission from group (g) and others (o).
lessons.explanation: Use go-w to ensure config files cannot be modified by other users.
lessons.instruction: Remove write permissions for group and others from secrets.txt.

lessons.title: 7. Check Ownership (ls -l)
lessons.definition: Every file has an owner user and group shown in ls -l output.
lessons.explanation: The 3rd and 4th columns of ls -l output show the owner user and group respectively.

lessons.title: 8. Create a Protected Directory (chmod 700)
lessons.definition: Mode 700 gives the owner full access to a directory while blocking all other users.
lessons.explanation: This is required for the ~/.ssh/ directory to ensure SSH security.
lessons.instruction: Create a directory named private_config and set it to mode 700.

exercises.title: Read the Permission String
exercises.problem: Run `ls -la /etc/passwd` and identify: is it a file or directory? Who is the owner? Can group members write to it?
exercises.hint: The first character is - (file) or d (directory). Column 3 is owner, column 4 is group. The 5th character in permissions is group write.
exercises.expected_result: It's a file (-), owned by root, and group cannot write to it.
exercises.objective: Practice reading and interpreting permission strings.

exercises.title: Create and Protect a Secrets File
exercises.problem: Create a file called `db_password.txt`, write `SUPER_SECRET_PASSWORD` into it, then set its permissions to 600.
exercises.hint: touch, then echo with >, then chmod 600.
exercises.objective: Practice file creation and applying restrictive permissions.

exercises.title: Create a Public Script
exercises.problem: Create a script `backup.sh` and set permissions so everyone can execute it, but only you can modify it.
exercises.hint: Use chmod 755 backup.sh. This gives owner rwx (7), group r-x (5), others r-x (5).
exercises.objective: Understand when to use 755 vs 700 for scripts.

exercises.title: Fix Permission Denied
exercises.problem: You have a script `run.sh` but get 'Permission denied' when executing it. What is wrong and how do you fix it?
exercises.hint: Run ls -l run.sh. If you see no x bit, the script is not executable. Fix with chmod +x run.sh.
exercises.expected_result: After chmod +x, ./run.sh executes without permission denied.
exercises.objective: Diagnose and fix missing execute permissions — a very common real-world issue.

exercises.title: Simulate SSH Key Security
exercises.problem: Create a directory `~/.ssh_test` and a file inside it called `id_test_rsa`. Apply correct SSH permissions: 700 for the directory, 600 for the private key file.
exercises.expected_result: ls -la ~/.ssh_test shows drwx------ for directory and -rw------- for key file.
exercises.objective: Understand why SSH enforces strict permissions on keys and directories.

exercises.title: Bulk Permission Reset
exercises.problem: Create a folder `webfiles/` with 3 html files inside. Set the directory to 755 and all files to 644.
exercises.hint: Use mkdir, touch three files, then `chmod 755 webfiles/` and `chmod 644 webfiles/*.html`.
exercises.expected_result: ls -la webfiles/ shows drwxr-xr-x for the folder and -rw-r--r-- for each html file.
exercises.objective: Practice setting permissions for a typical web content directory.

exercises.title: Lock Down a Config Directory
exercises.problem: Create a directory `app_secrets/` with three files inside. Use chmod -R 600 to restrict ALL of them. Then check: does the directory itself have the right permissions to enter with cd?
exercises.hint: chmod -R 600 sets files to 600 but also sets the directory to 600 (no execute bit = can't cd into it). Fix the directory with chmod 700 app_secrets/.
exercises.expected_result: Files are 600, directory is 700. You can cd into it but others cannot.
exercises.objective: Understand why directories need the execute bit (x) to allow cd, unlike files.

exercises.title: Diagnose a Web Server Permission Failure
exercises.problem: Simulate: create `/tmp/webroot/index.html` owned by root with mode 640. Why would nginx (running as www-data) fail to serve it? What chmod and chown commands fix it?
exercises.hint: 640 means others have no read. www-data is neither owner nor group, so it falls into 'others'. Fix: chmod 644 and chown www-data:www-data.
exercises.expected_result: After chmod 644, the 'others' group (which includes www-data) can read the file.
exercises.objective: Reason through permission failures in a web server context.

exercises.title: Write a Permission Audit Script
exercises.problem: Using echo and ls -l, create a mini security audit: check permissions on ~/.ssh (should be 700), ~/.bashrc (should be 644), and any .env file if it exists.
exercises.hint: Use ls -la ~/.ssh, ls -l ~/.bashrc, and ls -l .env. You can combine with echo statements for labeling.
exercises.objective: Simulate a real security audit process using basic Linux commands.

exercises.title: SUID Bit Investigation
exercises.problem: Run `ls -la /usr/bin/passwd`. You will see an 's' in the permissions. Look up what the SUID bit means and explain why /usr/bin/passwd needs it.
exercises.hint: The SUID bit (s instead of x for owner) means the program runs as the file owner (root) not the calling user. passwd needs root access to modify /etc/shadow.
exercises.expected_result: -rwsr-xr-x root root /usr/bin/passwd — the s means SUID.
exercises.objective: Explore advanced permission concepts used in system security.

quiz.question: What does the permission string `-rwxr-xr--` represent?
options: A directory with full access for everyone
A file where owner has rwx, group has r-x, others have r-- only
A file where everyone has full rwx access
A file where only the owner can read it
quiz.answer: A file where owner has rwx, group has r-x, others have r-- only
quiz.explanation: Reading left to right: - (file), rwx (owner), r-x (group), r-- (others).

incorrect_explanations.A directory with full access for everyon: Directories start with d. This starts with -, meaning it's a regular file.
incorrect_explanations.A file where everyone has full rwx acces: Others only have r-- (read only), not full rwx.
incorrect_explanations.A file where only the owner can read it: Group has r-x and others have r-- — they can also read.

quiz.question: What numeric permission is required for an SSH private key file?
quiz.explanation: SSH private keys must be 600 (rw-------). SSH client will refuse to use a key with looser permissions.

incorrect_explanations.777: 777 gives everyone read/write/execute — critically insecure for private keys.
incorrect_explanations.755: 755 gives group and others execute+read access — still too open for a private key.
incorrect_explanations.644: 644 lets group and others read your private key — SSH will refuse this.

quiz.question: Which command makes a script executable?
quiz.explanation: +x adds execute permission. Without it, trying to run the script gives 'Permission denied'.

incorrect_explanations.chmod 600 script.sh: 600 = rw------- — no execute bit, still not runnable.
incorrect_explanations.chmod -w script.sh: -w removes write permission — does nothing to add execute.

quiz.question: What is 644 in symbolic notation?
quiz.explanation: 6 = rw- (read+write), 4 = r-- (read only), 4 = r-- (read only). Owner can write, group and others can only read.

incorrect_explanations.rwxr-xr-x: That's 755 — common for directories and executable files.

quiz.question: You need group members to write to a shared directory, but others should have no access. What permission do you set?
quiz.explanation: 770 = rwxrwx--- — owner and group have full access, others have no access at all.

incorrect_explanations.chmod 775: 775 = rwxrwxr-x — others can read and execute, not what we want.
incorrect_explanations.chmod 744: 744 = rwxr--r-- — group can only read, not write.
incorrect_explanations.chmod 700: 700 = rwx------ — only the owner has access. Group has no access.

quiz.question: Which command changes the owner of a file to 'deploy_user'?
quiz.explanation: chown (change owner) changes the file's owner. chgrp changes only the group.

incorrect_explanations.chgrp deploy_user file: chgrp changes only the group, not the owner.
incorrect_explanations.usermod deploy_user file: usermod modifies user accounts, not file ownership.

quiz.question: What does the first character of a permission string tell you?
quiz.explanation: The first character is the file type: - = regular file, d = directory, l = symbolic link.

incorrect_explanations.The owner's read permission: Owner permissions start at position 2-4, not position 1.
incorrect_explanations.Whether the file is executable: Execute permission is shown by x in positions 4, 7, or 10.
incorrect_explanations.The file's group: The group is shown in the ls -l output as a separate column, not in the permission string.

quiz.question: A web server process running as 'www-data' cannot read /var/www/html/index.html. The file is owned by root with mode 640. What is the problem?
options: The file has no execute permission
www-data is in the 'others' category and 640 gives others no read permission
The file is a directory, not a regular file
quiz.answer: www-data is in the 'others' category and 640 gives others no read permission
quiz.explanation: 640 = rw-r----- — root (owner) has rw, root group has r, others have nothing. www-data is not root and likely not in root's group, so it falls into 'others' with no access.

incorrect_explanations.The file has no execute permission: HTML files don't need execute permission to be served by a web server.
incorrect_explanations.The web server needs chmod 777: Never use 777. The fix is chmod 644 or chown www-data:www-data.
incorrect_explanations.The file is a directory, not a regular f: We are dealing with a file (index.html), not a directory.

quiz.question: What does `chmod -R 755 /var/www/html` do?
options: Recursively sets 755 on the directory and all files inside it
quiz.answer: Recursively sets 755 on the directory and all files inside it
quiz.explanation: -R means recursive — it applies the permission change to the directory and every file/subdirectory within it.

incorrect_explanations.Changes permissions of /var/www/html onl: Without -R, chmod only affects the specified target. -R makes it recursive.
incorrect_explanations.Removes permissions from all files: 755 adds permissions (rwxr-xr-x), it does not remove them.
incorrect_explanations.Changes the owner recursively: chown -R changes ownership. chmod changes permissions.

quiz.question: Output prediction: After `chmod 750 mydir`, what is the ls -l output for mydir?
quiz.explanation: 750 = 7(rwx) owner, 5(r-x) group, 0(---) others. It's a directory so it starts with d.

incorrect_explanations.drwxr-xr-x: That's 755 — others have r-x access, but 750 gives others no access.
incorrect_explanations.drwx------: That's 700 — group also has no access, but 750 gives group r-x.
incorrect_explanations.drwxrwxr-x: That's 775 — group has write access, but 750 gives group only r-x.

quiz.question: Which directory permission is required for `cd mydir` to work?
quiz.explanation: The execute bit on a directory means 'can enter'. Without x, cd gives 'Permission denied' even if you have read access.

incorrect_explanations.Read (r): Read on a directory allows listing contents (ls), not entering (cd).
incorrect_explanations.Write (w): Write on a directory allows creating/deleting files inside — not entering.
incorrect_explanations.Both read and write (rw): You only need execute (x) to enter. Read is for listing contents.

quiz.question: What is the purpose of `chown -R www-data:www-data /var/www/myapp`?
options: Grant all users permission to read /var/www/myapp
Recursively transfer ownership of all files to www-data user and group
quiz.answer: Recursively transfer ownership of all files to www-data user and group
quiz.explanation: chown -R www-data:www-data changes the owner AND group of every file in the directory tree to www-data. This is done after deploying web app files so the web server can read them.

incorrect_explanations.Grant all users permission to read /var/: chown changes ownership, not permissions. Use chmod for permissions.
incorrect_explanations.Delete all files in /var/www/myapp: chown never deletes files. -R means recursive ownership change.
incorrect_explanations.Change file permissions to 777 recursive: chmod -R changes permissions. chown -R changes ownership.

resources.summary: Linux permissions control access through three levels (owner, group, others) and three types (read, write, execute). chmod changes permissions, chown changes ownership. Get permissions right and you prevent unauthorized access and deployment failures.

links.description: Interactive, beginner-friendly guide to Linux file permissions.

links.title: chmod Calculator
links.description: Visual tool to convert between numeric and symbolic permission modes.

resources.cheat_sheet:
# Linux Permissions Cheat Sheet

## Reading Permission Strings
-rwxr-xr--
│ │││ │││ └── Others: r-- = read only
│ │││ └────── Group:  r-x = read + execute
│ └────────── Owner:  rwx = full access
└──────────── Type:   - = file, d = directory

## chmod Symbolic Mode
chmod u+x file          # Add execute for owner
chmod go-w file         # Remove write from group+others
chmod a+r file          # Add read for all
chmod u=rwx,go=r file   # Set exact permissions

## chmod Numeric Mode
chmod 777 file          # rwxrwxrwx — NEVER in production
chmod 755 file          # rwxr-xr-x — scripts, directories
chmod 644 file          # rw-r--r-- — config files, web content
chmod 600 file          # rw------- — private keys, .env files
chmod 700 dir/          # rwx------ — private directories

## Common Permission Scenarios
.env files, private keys:     chmod 600
Shell scripts (public):       chmod 755
Shell scripts (private):      chmod 700
Web content files:            chmod 644
Web content directories:      chmod 755
SSH directory (~/.ssh):       chmod 700

## chown / chgrp
chown user file           # Change owner
chown user:group file     # Change owner and group
chown -R user:group dir/  # Recursive ownership change
chgrp group file          # Change group only

## Octal Reference
0 = ---   4 = r--
1 = --x   5 = r-x
2 = -w-   6 = rw-
3 = -wx   7 = rwx

commands_table.name: ls -l
commands_table.description: Show files with permissions, owner, and size.

commands_table.name: chmod +x
commands_table.description: Add execute permission to a file.

commands_table.name: chown
commands_table.description: Change file owner and optionally group.

commands_table.name: chgrp
commands_table.description: Change only the group of a file.

commands_table.name: stat
commands_table.description: Show detailed file info including numeric permissions.

revision_notes: Numbers: r=4, w=2, x=1. Add them: rwx=7, rw-=6, r-x=5, r--=4.
SSH private keys MUST be 600. SSH directories MUST be 700.
Web server files: directories 755, files 644, owned by web server user (www-data).
chmod -R applies recursively. Be careful — it changes both files AND directories.
best_practices: Never use 777 in production — it's a security vulnerability, not a fix.
Set .env and credential files to 600 immediately after creation.
Use chown -R to transfer web app files to the web server user after deployment.
Apply the principle of least privilege — give only the permissions needed, nothing more.
beginner_mistakes: Using chmod 777 to 'fix' permission errors — this removes all security boundaries.
Forgetting -R with chown/chmod on directories — only the directory itself changes.
Setting execute bit on config files — configs don't need to be executable.
Leaving .env files at 644 — they should be 600 if they contain secrets.

interview_questions.question: What permissions should an SSH private key have and why?
interview_questions.answer: 600 (rw-------). Only the owner needs to read/write the key. SSH client enforces this and refuses to use keys with looser permissions to prevent other users from stealing credentials.

interview_questions.question: What is the difference between chmod 755 and chmod 644?
interview_questions.answer: 755 (rwxr-xr-x) includes execute permission — used for scripts and directories. 644 (rw-r--r--) has no execute — used for regular files like configs and web content.

interview_questions.question: Why does a directory need the execute bit (x) to allow cd?
interview_questions.answer: On directories, execute means 'can traverse/enter'. Without x, cd gives Permission Denied. Read (r) only allows listing with ls, not entering.

books.title: The Linux Command Line — William Shotts
books.description: Excellent coverage of permissions, users, and file ownership in chapters 9-10.

books.title: Linux System Administration — Tom Adelstein
books.description: In-depth guide to user management, permissions, and security in production Linux systems.
